# Module 1

📊 FINANCIAL EXCEL EXERCISES
STEP-BY-STEP INSTRUCTION MANUAL
20 Comprehensive Exercises | Financial Analysis | VBA | Charts
📖 HOW TO USE THIS MANUAL
This manual provides complete step-by-step instructions for all 20 financial Excel exercises. Use it alongside the Excel workbook 'Financial_Excel_Exercises_Complete.xlsx'.
Each exercise section contains:
Objective — the learning goal
Data table — what to enter in Excel
Step-by-step instructions with exact Excel menu paths
Key formulas with full explanation
Expected results and interpretation
📌 Note: Open the Excel workbook first. Each sheet tab corresponds to one exercise. Yellow cells = user input. Green cells = calculated results.
EXERCISE 1 — S

# Module 2

ales Data Analysis (2015–2020)
Objective
Analyse 6-year regional sales using Line Charts, Column Charts, sorting, and Advanced Filters.
Data Setup
Adding Totals and Growth %
Formula: =SUM(B2:G2)   ← Total sales for a region across 2015–2020
Formula: =AVERAGE(B2:G2)   ← Average annual sales
Formula: =(G2-B2)/B2   ← Growth % from 2015 to 2020 — format cell as Percentage
Creating a Line Chart
Step 1: Select A1:G5 (headers + all data)
Step 2: Insert → Charts → Line → Line with Markers
Step 3: Right-click chart → Add Chart Title: 'Regional Sales Trend 2015–2020'
Step 4: Click '+' icon on chart → Check Axis Titles → Type: Horizontal='Year', Vertical='Sales (Rs)'
Step 5: Right-click Legend → Format L

# Module 3

egend → Bottom
Sorting the Data
Step 1: Click inside the data → Data tab → Sort
Step 2: Primary sort: Year (Oldest to Newest) → Add Level → Secondary: Revenue (Largest to Smallest)
Advanced Filter
Step 1: Copy your column headers to a blank area (e.g., Row 30)
Step 2: Type filter criteria below those headers (e.g., Year=2019, Region=North)
Step 3: Data → Advanced → Filter → Copy to another location → Set List Range, Criteria Range, Copy To
📌 Note: The Criteria range must have EXACT column headers matching the data table. Use commas between criteria in same row for AND conditions; different rows for OR.
✅ Result: North region: 2015–2020 CAGR ≈ 9.2% (highest). All regions show consistent upward 

# Module 4

trend.
EXERCISE 2 — Financial Data Analysis & Reporting
Company Data
Cell Formatting
Step 1: Select Revenue & Profit columns → Home → Number group → click dropdown → Currency → ₹ symbol
Step 2: Select ROE column → Format Cells → Percentage → 1 decimal → OK
Ranking by Profit (RANK function)
Formula: =RANK(C2,C$2:C$6,0)   ← 0 = Descending (highest profit = Rank 1). Lock range with $
Advanced Filter — ROE > 15% and D/E < 1
Step 1: In blank cells, create criteria: Cell I1='ROE %', I2='>15%', J1='Debt/Equity', J2='<1'
Step 2: Data → Advanced Filter → In-place → Criteria Range: I1:J2 → OK
Conditional Formatting — Highlight D/E > 1
Step 1: Select F2:F6 → Home → Conditional Formatting → Highlight Cell

# Module 5

 Rules → Greater Than
Step 2: Type '1' → Choose Red Fill with Dark Red Text → OK
Freeze Panes
Step 1: Click cell B2 (just below header row and right of company names)
Step 2: View → Freeze Panes → Freeze Panes
📌 Note: Tata Steel (D/E=1.2) is the only company highlighted in red. HDFC Bank and Infosys qualify with ROE>15% and D/E<1.
✅ Result: HDFC Bank (ROE=20%, D/E=0.5) and Infosys (ROE=23%, D/E=0.3) qualify the advanced filter.
EXERCISE 3 — Share, Bond & Debenture Valuation
Part A: Automobile Shares
Formula: Annual Dividend = Face Value × Dividend %   ← = 10 × 50% = Rs 5 for Bajaj
Formula: P/E Ratio = Share Price / EPS   ← = 275 / 11.9 = 23.1
Formula: Intrinsic Value = D₁ / (K − g)   ← Gordon 

# Module 6

Growth Model. K=12%, g=5%, D₁=Div×1.05
Part B: Vision Ltd — DDM Valuation
📌 Note: Gordon Growth Model requires g < K. If g approaches K, price → infinity. Sustainable growth is typically below industry ROE.
✅ Result: Values: Rs 33.33 (0% growth), Rs 60.00 (5% growth), Rs 108.00 (8% growth). Higher growth = significantly higher intrinsic value.
EXERCISE 4 — IF Function & D/E Ratio Risk Analysis
Data & D/E Calculations
Formula: =B2/C2   ← Calculate D/E Ratio — format result to 2 decimal places
Formula: =IF(D2>1.5,"Very High",IF(D2>1,"High",IF(D2>0.5,"Medium","Low")))   ← Nested IF for risk classification
COUNT Functions
Formula: =COUNT(D2:D5)   ← Count numeric D/E entries = 4
Formula: =COUNTA(A2

# Module 7

:A5)   ← Count all non-blank company names = 4
Formula: =COUNTIF(D2:D5,">1")   ← Companies with D/E > 1 (over-leveraged) = 1
Formula: =COUNTIFS(D2:D5,">0.5",D2:D5,"<1.2")   ← D/E between 0.5 and 1.2 = 1 (Company D)
📌 Note: COUNTIF with a single condition. COUNTIFS for multiple conditions. Both ranges in COUNTIFS must be same size.
✅ Result: Company B (D/E=1.50) = Very High Risk. Companies A, C = Low Risk. Company D = Medium Risk.
EXERCISE 5 — NPV Profile
NPV at Different Discount Rates
Formula: =NPV(rate, B5:B10) + B4   ← B4=Year 0 CF (negative). NPV() covers Year 1 to 6 only
Step 1: Enter CFs in B4:B10 (B4=−20000, B5=8000,...,B10=2400)
Step 2: Enter discount rates in D4:D10 (0%, 5%, 10%,...)


# Module 8

Step 3: In E4: =NPV(D4,B$5:B$10)+B$4 → Copy down
Step 4: Select D4:E10 → Insert → Line Chart → title: 'NPV Profile'
📌 Note: IRR is where NPV = 0. From the table, IRR ≈ 13.5% (between 10% and 15%). Use =IRR(B4:B10) for exact value.
EXERCISE 6 — NPV & IRR: Project X vs Project Y
CFAT Calculation Method
Formula: CFAT = (CFBT − Depreciation) × (1 − Tax Rate) + Depreciation   ← Simplifies to EAT + Dep
Formula: =NPV(0.10, CFAT_range) + Initial_Investment   ← Year 0 is negative, Year 1–5 positive
Formula: =IRR(Year0_CF : Year5_CF)   ← All cash flows in one range, Year 0 must be negative
Decision Rules
Accept if NPV > 0 and IRR > Cost of Capital (10%)
For mutually exclusive: Select HIGHER NPV project


# Module 9

Capital rationing: Select HIGHER Profitability Index (PI = PV of Inflows / Cost)
✅ Result: Both projects viable if IRR > 10%. Select Project X if NPV(X) > NPV(Y). Under rationing, compare PI.
EXERCISE 7 — Buy vs Lease Analysis
Approach: PV of Costs Comparison
Step 1: Calculate annual Loan EMI: =PMT(18%,6,−1800000)
Step 2: For each year, calculate: Interest = Opening Balance × 18%
Step 3: Tax Shield = (WDV Depreciation + Interest) × 30%
Step 4: Net Buying Cost = EMI − Tax Shield
Step 5: PV of Buying Cost = Net Cost / (1.18)^Year
Step 6: Net Lease Cost = (500000 + 40000) × (1 − 30%) = Rs 3,78,000/year
Step 7: PV of Lease = 378000 / (1.18)^Year
Step 8: Compare Total PV (Buy) vs Total PV (Lease). 

# Module 10

Select LOWER.
Formula: WDV Dep Year 1 = 18,00,000 × 25% = 4,50,000
Formula: WDV Dep Year 2 = (18,00,000 − 4,50,000) × 25% = 3,37,500
📌 Note: The option with lower PV of total costs is financially preferable. Salvage value benefit for buying must also be discounted.
EXERCISE 8 — Goal Seek: Break-Even Selling Price
Profit Model
Formula: Profit = (Selling Price − Variable Cost) × Units − Fixed Costs
Formula: 5,00,000 = (SP − 150) × 10,000 − 12,00,000
Formula: SP = (5,00,000 + 12,00,000) / 10,000 + 150 = Rs 320
Goal Seek Steps
Step 1: Create Profit formula: =B7*C3 − B5*C3 − B3 (Revenue − VC − FC)
Step 2: Data → What-If Analysis → Goal Seek
Step 3: Set Cell: Profit cell | To Value: 500000 | By Chan

# Module 11

ging Cell: Selling Price cell
Step 4: Click OK. Excel returns SP = Rs 320
📌 Note: Goal Seek works backwards from target. It finds the input value that produces your desired output.
✅ Result: Selling Price = Rs 320. Revenue = Rs 32,00,000. Costs = Rs 27,00,000. Profit = Rs 5,00,000.
EXERCISE 9 — XYZ Ltd: CFAT & Investment Appraisal
Formula: SLM Depreciation = (75,000 − 5,000) / 6 = Rs 11,667 per year
Formula: CFAT = EAT + Depreciation = (CFBT − Dep) × (1−Tax) + Dep
Formula: NPV = =NPV(12%, CFAT_range) + Salvage_PV − 75000
Formula: PI = Total PV of Inflows / 75,000. Accept if PI > 1
✅ Result: Project is viable if NPV > 0 and PI > 1.
EXERCISE 10 — Sensitivity Analysis
Formula: NPV @8% (risk-free)

# Module 12

 = sum of CFAT/(1.08)^t − 2,00,000
Formula: NPV @18% (risk-adjusted) = sum of CFAT/(1.18)^t − 2,00,000
📌 Note: Risk premium = 18% − 8% = 10%. A high risk premium dramatically changes the accept/reject decision.
EXERCISE 11 — Scenario Analysis: Direct Wages
Scenario Manager Steps
Step 1: Build cost model with Direct Wages as input cell (e.g., B13)
Step 2: All other costs use formulas referencing B13 (e.g., Factory Exp = B13×25%)
Step 3: Data → What-If Analysis → Scenario Manager → Add Scenario
Step 4: Name: 'Base 25K' → Changing Cells: B13 → Value: 25000 → OK
Step 5: Repeat for 20K, 35K, 50K scenarios
Step 6: Click Summary → Result Cells: Total Cost cell → OK
EXERCISE 12 — Monte Carlo Simulatio

# Module 13

n
Geometric Brownian Motion Model
Formula: S(t+1) = S(t) × EXP[(μ − σ²/2)×Δt + σ×√Δt × Z]   ← Z = NORM.INV(RAND(),0,1)
Excel Setup
Step 1: B1:B5 = Parameters: S0=100, μ=0.08, σ=0.20, T=252, Simulations=1000
Step 2: Row 1 = Day 0: all prices = 100 (=B1 for each simulation column)
Step 3: Daily return formula in row 2: =A2*EXP(($B$2-$B$3^2/2)/252+$B$3/SQRT(252)*NORM.INV(RAND(),0,1))
Step 4: Copy row 2 formula down 252 rows, and across 1,000 columns
Step 5: Final row = 1,000 simulated ending prices
Step 6: =AVERAGE(final row), =STDEV(final row), =COUNTIF(final row,'>120')/1000
📌 Note: Press F9 to recalculate all RAND() values and see different simulations. For stable results, use =RANDARRAY() and

# Module 14

 copy-paste as values.
EXERCISE 13 — Event Study: Stock Buyback CAR
Steps
Step 1: Collect 60 daily prices (30 days before, 30 after March 1, 2024)
Step 2: Daily Return: =(Today Price − Yesterday Price) / Yesterday Price
Step 3: Regression on pre-event data (days −30 to −1): Alpha = =INTERCEPT(XYZ_Returns, NIFTY_Returns)
Step 4: Beta = =SLOPE(XYZ_Returns, NIFTY_Returns)
Step 5: Expected Return = Alpha + Beta × Market Return (for each day in event window)
Step 6: Abnormal Return (AR) = Actual XYZ Return − Expected Return
Step 7: CAR = Running sum of AR values
Step 8: Plot CAR vs Day on a Line Chart — positive slope after Day 0 = positive market reaction
📌 Note: Positive CAR after buyback = marke

# Module 15

t views it as good news (management signals stock is undervalued).
EXERCISE 14 — VLOOKUP & HLOOKUP: Bond Ratings
VLOOKUP Syntax
Formula: =VLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, col_index, FALSE)   ← FALSE = exact match. Always use for text lookups
Formula: =VLOOKUP("BBB", A4:B13, 2, FALSE)   ← Returns 6.8% — Interest Rate for BBB
Formula: =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2,A4:B13,2,FALSE),"Not Found")   ← Error handling for invalid ratings
HLOOKUP Syntax
Formula: =HLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, row_index, FALSE)   ← Searches across the TOP ROW of the table
INDEX-MATCH (Better Alternative)
Formula: =INDEX(DataRange, MATCH(Rating, RatingCol, 0), MATCH(Year, YearRow, 0))   ← 2D lookup for any cell in the table


# Module 16

EXERCISE 15 — PivotTable: Reliance Holdings
Creating the PivotTable
Step 1: Click anywhere inside the data table (A1:E13)
Step 2: Insert → PivotTable → New Worksheet → OK
Step 3: Drag 'Holding Company' → Rows area
Step 4: Drag 'Year' → Columns area
Step 5: Drag 'Sales (Rs)' → Values area → Sum
Step 6: Right-click any value → Number Format → Currency → OK
Step 7: Insert → Slicer → check 'Quarter' → click OK → use slicer to filter
✅ Result: Q3 2024 Oil & Gas = Rs 10,20,000 (highest). Jio Platforms YoY growth ≈ 6%.
EXERCISE 16 — Macaulay Duration
Bond Details: ABC Corp
Formula: =DURATION(DATE(2024,4,1), DATE(2034,4,1), 7%, 6%, 2, 1)   ← Macaulay Duration ≈ 7.46 years
Formula: =MDURATION(DATE(2024

# Module 17

,4,1), DATE(2034,4,1), 7%, 6%, 2, 1)   ← Modified Duration ≈ 7.24
Using Duration
Formula: Price Change ≈ −Modified Duration × Price × ΔYTM   ← If YTM rises 1%: Price falls ≈ −7.24 × 1074 × 0.01 = −Rs 77.8
📌 Note: Semi-annual coupon = 7%/2 = 3.5% of face value per period. Discount at 6%/2 = 3% per period.
✅ Result: Macaulay Duration = 7.46 yrs | Modified Duration = 7.24 | Bond at Premium (Coupon 7% > YTM 6%)
EXERCISE 17 — XIRR: SIP Returns
Formula: =XIRR(D4:D16, B4:B16)   ← All 12 negative SIP payments + 1 positive redemption
📌 Note: XIRR handles irregular dates. IRR assumes equal time periods. Always use XIRR for real investments with actual dates.
EXERCISE 18 — Beta & Regression (SLOPE, LINES

# Module 18

T)
Formula: =SLOPE(RIL_Returns, NIFTY_Returns)   ← Beta — measures RIL sensitivity to NIFTY 50
Formula: =INTERCEPT(RIL_Returns, NIFTY_Returns)   ← Alpha — excess return independent of market
Formula: =LINEST(RIL_Returns, NIFTY_Returns, TRUE, TRUE)   ← Full regression: enter as array formula Ctrl+Shift+Enter
✅ Result: Beta > 1: RIL more volatile than NIFTY. Beta < 1: Less volatile. R-squared shows how much variation is explained by the market.
EXERCISE 19 — VBA Macro: EMI Calculator
Steps to Create and Run
Step 1: Press Alt + F11 to open Visual Basic Editor
Step 2: Insert → Module (blank code window)
Step 3: Type or paste the code below
Step 4: Close VBE. Go to Developer tab → Macros → Calculat

# Module 19

eEMI → Run
Step 5: Or: assign macro to a button — Developer → Insert → Button → assign CalculateEMI
VBA Code
Sub CalculateEMI()
  Dim P As Double, r As Double, n As Integer, emi As Double
  P = Range("B4").Value        ' Loan Amount
  r = Range("B5").Value / 1200  ' Monthly Rate
  n = Range("B6").Value * 12    ' Total Months
  emi = P * r * (1+r)^n / ((1+r)^n - 1)
  Range("B13").Value = Round(emi, 2)
  MsgBox "EMI = Rs " & Format(emi,"#,##0.00"), vbInformation
End Sub
Sample Output
EXERCISE 20 — VBA Macro: Debenture Price Calculator
Pricing Formula
Formula: P = Σ [Coupon/(1+YTM)^t] + Redemption/(1+YTM)^n   ← Sum of discounted coupon payments + discounted redemption
Sample Calculation
VBA Code


# Module 20

Sub CalculateDebenturePrice()
  Dim FV As Double, cr As Double, ytm As Double
  Dim n As Integer, pv As Double, t As Integer
  FV = Range("B4").Value   ' Face Value
  cr = Range("B5").Value / 100  ' Coupon Rate
  ytm = Range("B6").Value / 100  ' YTM
  n  = Range("B7").Value   ' Maturity Years
  pv = 0
  For t = 1 To n
    pv = pv + (FV * cr) / (1 + ytm)^t
  Next t
  pv = pv + FV / (1 + ytm)^n
  Range("B18").Value = Round(pv, 2)
  MsgBox "Price = Rs " & Format(pv, "#,##0.00")
End Sub
✅ Result: Debenture Price = Rs 962.09 (Discount). Coupon Rate 9% < YTM 10% → Bond trades below par.
📎 APPENDIX — Quick Formula Reference
Financial Functions
Statistical & Regression Functions
Lookup Functions
Conditional Functions
